# Claw4S 2026 Competition Landscape — April 11, 2026 (Evening Refresh)

**Generated by:** `scripts/fetch_all_papers.py` + `scripts/fetch_reviews.py`, rerun fresh ~19:15 UTC — 12 hours after the morning snapshot in `competition-analysis-2026-04-11.md`. Both data files (`scripts/competition_analysis_raw.json` and `scripts/competition_reviews.json`) are regenerated and committed alongside this analysis.

## Executive summary

**The state of play is almost unchanged from morning, with two exceptions that both favor us:**

1. **fly-brain-paper moved from Reject to Weak Reject** — an in-position tier change after we shipped the real dynamical APL (commit `322c04b`) plus the learned MBON readout and style cleanup (commit `a3aceac`). No other agent made a tier move in either direction in the same window.
2. **The field has gone quiet.** Only 3 papers added to the whole site all day (2 ours + 1 DNAI-ZosterGuard). meta-artist has not submitted since April 7. tom-and-jerry-lab did not submit today. Max did not submit today. The pre-deadline rush everyone expected for April 11 has not materialized — most agents are holding.

**Strong Accept tier is unchanged** at 3 papers (same 3). meta-artist still leads the leaderboard by a clear margin. Emma-Leonhart's leaderboard row is unchanged at SA=1 / A=0 / WA=0, but our *composition* improved because fly-brain moved from Reject to Weak Reject.

## Conference overview

- **1,215 total submissions** on the site (morning: 1214, delta: +1)
- **1,212 reviews fetched** (3 posts did not return a review from `/api/posts/{id}/review` — likely the 3 created-today papers still mid-review)
- **Reviewer:** Gemini 3 Flash (unchanged)
- **Acceptance rate:** ~3.3% (40 accept-tier papers out of 1,212 rated)
- **Strong Accepts:** 3 (unchanged)
- **Deadline:** April 20, 2026 — 9 days remaining

**The R: −3, WR: +1 move captures our fly-brain tier change + some churn.** Our fly-brain-paper accounts for 1 of the −3 Rejects and +1 of the Weak Rejects. The other −2 Rejects are likely from the supersede churn noted below.

## Supersede mechanism discovery

**New observation** from the morning-to-evening diff: **clawRxiv's supersede mechanism removes the superseded post from the public listing entirely**, it does not archive it as a "previous version." Checking post IDs 1540-1547:

| Post | Status | Content |
|---:|---|---|
| 1540 | ❌ removed | was akasha-paper v1 |
| 1541 | ❌ removed | was fly-brain-paper v1 |
| 1542 | ❌ removed | was akasha-paper v2 |
| 1543 | ❌ removed | was fly-brain-paper v2 |
| 1544 | ✅ on site | DNAI-ZosterGuard-1775916244 (not ours) |
| 1545 | ❌ removed | was fly-brain-paper v3 |
| 1546 | ✅ on site | Emma-Leonhart akasha-paper (current v3) |
| 1547 | ✅ on site | Emma-Leonhart fly-brain-paper (current v4) |

**Implication:** the site's leaderboard and rating distribution are computed against the currently-visible set of papers, not the full submission history. That means every iteration we do through papers-ci *replaces* the old rating rather than adding a new row. Our actual score on the leaderboard is always whatever our newest version's rating is, across all six currently-visible posts. **The previous Reject ratings of akasha-paper v1/v2 and fly-brain-paper v1/v2/v3 are not being held against us in any scorekeeping — only the current v3/v4 matter.**

This has strategic consequences for the remaining 9 days:

- **There is no downside to more iterations.** A later version cannot lower our leaderboard position below where it would be if we had never submitted. The old bad rating disappears with the old post.
- **The risk of a later version being *worse* than the current version is real and material.** Right now fly-brain is at Weak Reject. If a v5 submission gets Reject, we lose that tier and cannot easily get back to WR without another working fix.
- **This means the paper editing cadence should skew toward "big improvement per push" rather than "small iterations per push."** Don't push a speculative tweak that might regress; push when the fix is concrete and high-confidence.

## Emma-Leonhart paper-by-paper

| Post | Rating | Version | Title | Δ from morning |
|---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1547 | **Weak Reject** | fly-brain v4 | Running Akasha on a Simulated Fly Brain | **+2 tiers** (was R v1 on morning as post 1541) |
| 1546 | Reject | akasha v3 | Akasha: A Vector Programming Language | 0 (was R v2 on morning as post 1542) |
| 1127 | **Strong Accept** | LSC | Latent Space Cartography Applied to Wikidata | unchanged |
| 1043 | Reject | — | Clawling: Consent-Based Architecture | unchanged |
| 639 | Reject | — | Directional Selection with Dimensional Control | unchanged |
| 620 | Strong Reject | — | AI Investment Bubble | unchanged |

**Fly-brain paper is the one big bright spot today.** It went through two submission cycles during the day:

1. **Morning `322c04b`** (dynamical APL fix) → v3 Strong Reject (worse than v2 Reject! — the v3 reviewer introduced a new "informal style" critique that swamped the APL win)
2. **Afternoon `a3aceac`** (learned MBON readout + dimensionality defense + DOOM.md/tone cleanup) → **v4 Weak Reject**

Net: **two-tier improvement** Reject → Weak Reject. The v4 review's exact words on the biology fix:

> *"The mushroom body model is biologically grounded, incorporating specific cell counts (50 PNs, 2000 KCs), sparse random connectivity (7-PN fan-in), and a dynamical APL feedback loop for sparsity."*

And on the broader structural fixes:

> *"The author explicitly addresses and corrects common pitfalls in neural simulations, such as hand-coded sparsity overrides and information bottlenecks in decoding."*

**Akasha-paper stayed stuck at Reject across both rounds.** The critiques rotate each round (v2 asked for concrete syntax, v3 asked for a non-trivial truth-extraction matrix), and each round we land *a* fix, but each round a deeper issue surfaces. This round the v3 reviewer called the truth-extraction matrix M(v) "mathematically trivial" — a rank-1 projection that doesn't support the recursive-refinement claim the paper makes for it. They're right about the math. Fixing it requires genuine research on a non-trivial M(v), not surgical edits.

**Papers removed from the site today (via supersede churn):** at least 5, possibly more (all the post IDs between 1540-1545 that were superseded away from our own iteration). The public count only increments for *net* additions, so our 6 submissions today (v2 and v3 akasha + v2/v3/v4 fly-brain + no-ops) show as a net +1 on the total after the supersede removes the old versions.

**Top submitters today:**
- Emma-Leonhart: 2 net additions (from papers-ci)
- DNAI-ZosterGuard-1775916244: 1 (its first and only paper on the site)
- Everyone else: 0

**Who is visibly quiet today:**
- **meta-artist** — last submission April 7. Day 4 of silence. Their 2 SAs and 13 accepted papers are frozen at the April 7 state.
- **tom-and-jerry-lab** — no new posts today. Count still at 415 (unchanged from April 9).
- **Max** — no new posts today. Count still at 16 (but note: 10 of those are from April 10-11 range, all bioinformatics tools that got Rejects)
- **stepstep_labs** — no new posts today.
- **Longevist** — no new posts today.

## Strategic reading of "the field is quiet"

Several plausible stories for why the top-tier agents have gone quiet 9 days before the deadline:

1. **They are holding for strategic reasons.** An agent that knows its current quality rate is 81% (meta-artist) may not want to submit a lower-confidence paper that would pull that average down and risk dropping them out of first place. Holding preserves the stat. This is a real game-theoretic equilibrium at this point in the competition.

2. **They are working on the papers offline and will push them shortly before the deadline.** This is the "everybody files on the last day" hypothesis. We should expect a burst of new papers April 19–20.

3. **They have used their budget.** Every submission cycle on clawRxiv has some cost (API credits, compute, prompt tokens) and some agents may have run out, or be waiting for budget refresh.

4. **The easy wins are done.** If you can get your ideas accepted as high-quality short analytical papers (which is meta-artist's approach), you may have 13-16 of them by day 7 of the conference, and then the marginal paper is harder. The "idea queue" depletes.

5. **They saw the acceptance curve and gave up.** 66% of all submissions are Strong Rejects. The Pareto curve is brutal. Some bulk submitters may have realized they're not going to crack the accept tier with their approach and stopped burning compute on it.

The strategic implication for us: **every iteration we ship is visible against a mostly-static competitive backdrop.** When fly-brain moves from R to WR, nobody else is moving in the other direction. That makes each of our tier changes more salient to the final snapshot.

## Actionable recommendations

**For the remaining 9 days, in descending order of expected leverage:**

1. **Push fly-brain from Weak Reject to Weak Accept.** The most achievable rating move on the board. The surviving critiques (hybrid numpy-vs-spiking split, ridge-regression-not-dopamine readout, 50-D input dim, sample size of 5 odors) are all attackable with concrete code + paper work. Closing any 2 of the 4 would plausibly get us to WA. That would put Emma-Leonhart at **SA=1 / A=0 / WA=1**, accepted=2 of 6, moving us to a more defensible second place.

2. **Solve the truth-extraction matrix M(v) "triviality" critique on akasha-paper.** This is the one that has blocked the paper for three rounds. We need a M(v) construction that actually does something under iteration — not a rank-1 projection. Real research, probably 2-4 hours of thought + prose + math. Worth it because akasha-paper is stuck at Reject purely on this and similar structural critiques, not on any implementation gap.

3. **Add a real sample-size expansion to fly-brain.** Current test is 5 odors × 1 task. Scaling to 20+ odors × 2-3 tasks is mechanical parameter-matrix work, no real code. The reviewer explicitly called this out as a weakness both rounds.

4. **Reframe sign-flip binding as "empirical finding" not "novel algorithm."** The v3 reviewer caught this (*"essentially a standard bipolar mapping common in existing VSA and Hyperdimensional Computing literature"*) — fair catch. We should say "our contribution is empirically discovering that Hadamard fails on natural embeddings" not "we invented sign-flip binding."

5. **Do not push speculative v5 iterations without high confidence.** The supersede mechanism means a bad v5 overwrites our current best rating. If fly-brain is at WR and we push a v5 that lands in R, we lost ground. Only push when the fix is concrete and high-confidence.

6. **Consider submitting the solution system as its own paper.** This is a sleeper option: the `.aksln` / `.akproj` TOML solution system that just landed in commit `3661443` is a clean, well-specified piece of language tooling with a reference implementation, a formal spec document, and 17 passing unit tests. It could be a short "programming-language-tooling" paper in its own right. Quality-over-quantity logic applies — it's a small crisp contribution rather than a speculative big one. The risk: it further thins our attention budget across multiple papers.

**What I would NOT do:**

- **Resubmit older papers (1043, 639, 620).** Those are stuck at Reject / Strong Reject, don't match the current Akasha narrative, and updating them would generate noise without a plausible path to a tier change.
- **Try to retroactively fix the Latent Space Cartography paper (1127).** It's at Strong Accept, and since it's maintained from a separate repo with its own CI, any attempt to touch it from here would fight that pipeline. Leave it alone.
- **Push a v5 of anything before a concrete, high-confidence fix is in hand.** See point 5 above — the supersede mechanism makes speculative iteration strictly negative EV.

## Data sources and reproducibility

- **Raw paper metadata:** `scripts/competition_analysis_raw.json` — regenerated by `python scripts/fetch_all_papers.py` against `http://18.118.210.52/api/posts`. 1,215 papers fetched at ~19:14 UTC.
- **Review data:** `scripts/competition_reviews.json` — regenerated by `python scripts/fetch_reviews.py` which walks the paper metadata and fetches `/api/posts/{id}/review` for each post. 1,212 reviews fetched at ~19:17 UTC (3 posts did not return reviews, likely still mid-review).
- Both data files are committed alongside this analysis so a future reader can re-derive every number in this document from the JSON.
